title: Chapter 5: Algorithm & Blues
content: I type it out like I'm defusing a bomb.

"Sometimes I feel like I'm not even in my own body anymore. Like I'm watching someone else live my life through a screen. Does anyone else feel hollow? Not sad. Not depressed. Just… empty in a way that has a shape. Like something used to be there and now there's just an outline."
My thumb hovers over Post. The words are ugly. Real. They smell like 2 AM and the kind of honesty you immediately regret. I post it anyway.
The upload wheel spins.
And spins.
And spins.
Then the text reloads.
"Grateful for every single one of you. 💫 The journey is just beginning."

I stare at my phone. I didn't write that. I didn't write a single word of that. I hit delete so fast my thumb cracks against the screen. My heart is doing something stupid in my chest — that arrhythmic panic of watching someone put words in your mouth while you stand there, mouth open, soundless.
I type it again. Slower this time, like the app might not notice if I'm careful.
"I feel hollow. Something is wrong with me. Something is wrong with all of this."
Post.
The wheel spins.
"Grateful for every single one of you. 💫 The journey is just beginning."
Same exact text. Same emoji. Same nothing. The app isn't broken. It's working perfectly. It's just not working for me.
I delete the post. I close the app. I reopen it. I type three words: "I am scared."
Post.
"Grateful for every single one of you. 💫 The journey is just beginning."

The Mephisto app doesn't allow unfiltered content. That's not a bug. That's the whole point. The contract didn't just take my empathy and my trust. It took my right to be messy in public. To be human in public. The algorithm decides what Chloe Hart feels, and Chloe Hart feels grateful. Always grateful. Forever grateful. 💫
I throw my phone onto the couch. It bounces twice and lands face-up, the notification light pulsing like a heartbeat that isn't mine.
The bathroom mirror is honest. That's the problem.

I stand there in the bad fluorescent light of my apartment — the one I keep meaning to replace with something warmer, something that doesn't make me look like a crime scene photo — and I study my face. My actual face. The one in the mirror.

It's the same as always. Mostly. Round cheeks that I've hated since middle school. The slight asymmetry in my eyebrows — the left one arches higher when I'm tired, which is always. The chin scar from when I was nine and thought I could skateboard off the garage roof. Same Chloe. Same face I've been dragging through New York for twenty-two years.
But then I open my phone camera and point it at myself.

The woman on the screen has higher cheekbones. Not contour — I know what contour looks like. The bone structure itself has been adjusted. Sharper. More architectural. Her skin is smoother, poreless, like she was rendered instead of born. Her eyes are slightly wider, the shade of green dialed up two notches into something cinematic. She's beautiful in a way that I am not.

I flip between mirror and phone. Mirror and phone. Mirror and phone. It's one of those moments where you can feel your brain trying to reconcile two incompatible truths and the gears are just grinding, grinding, grinding.

My real face hasn't changed. My body hasn't changed. But every image I produce — every photo, every video, every story — auto-tunes me into someone else. The algorithm isn't just filtering my words. It's filtering my face. My digital self is being optimized, and my digital self is the only self that five million people can see.
I am becoming someone else's idea of me.

And the worst part — the part that sits in my chest like a swallowed battery — is that her face is better than mine. Objectively. Demonstrably. Two million people can't be wrong, right? Isn't that what the numbers say?
I put the phone down. The mirror keeps showing me the truth, but nobody's looking at the mirror.

I set up the canvas in the corner of the living room where the light is best — morning sun through dirty windows, the kind of light that makes everything look like a Vermeer painting if you squint. I squeeze cadmium red onto the palette. The smell hits me immediately: that sharp, mineral, slightly toxic tang of oil paint that I've loved since I was fifteen and stole my first tube from Pearl Paint on Canal Street. God, I miss that store. Everything real in this city keeps closing.

I haven't painted in weeks. Not since the deal. My hands are hungry for it — the drag of the brush, the resistance of the canvas, the way paint covers your cuticles and stays there for days like evidence that you made something.
I'm three strokes in when the notification hits.
[Traditional media performance is 94% less engaging than digital. Switch to recommended template?]

The keyboard is right there. One tap and the app will give me a professionally curated, algorithm-optimized digital art template that will probably get fifty times the engagement. I can almost feel it suggesting. Almost hear the invisible sale.
I ignore it. I keep painting.
But something is wrong with my hand.

I'm trying to do what I always do — lay down thick, ugly, emotional strokes, let the paint be paint, let it be raw and uncomfortable. Instead, my hand moves in smooth, confident arcs. Clean lines. Balanced composition. My brush finds the most aesthetically pleasing angle automatically, like muscle memory has been replaced by a style guide.

I try to paint something messy. Something that hurts. My hand won't do it. It keeps producing these elegant, market-ready marks that look like they belong on a coffee brand's Instagram instead of in the gut of a woman who feels like she's being erased in real time.

The paint smells the same. The canvas feels the same. But the thing that moves through me into the brush — that raw, ugly, beautiful instinct I've been counting on my whole life — it's been overwritten. Like a software update I didn't consent to.
I stare at the canvas. It's technically perfect. It's completely dead.

I push the notification away and paint over it. I paint over it three more times until the canvas is a muddy, beautiful mess and my hand is shaking and the apartment smells like mineral spirits and something that might be rage.
The app sends another notification: [Content quality below threshold. Engagement projection: negative. Recommend undo?]
I don't answer it.
The selfie is an accident. I'm checking if there's paint on my face after cleaning up, and I catch my own reflection in the camera and think — fine. Let's see what she looks like today.
Click.

The app processes. I watch it happen. My cheekbones rise by three pixels. My teeth lighten two shades. The crease between my eyebrows — the one my mom has too, the one that says "I'm thinking and I'm not sorry about it" — smooths out entirely. My expression shifts from thinking to mysterious. From present to performative.
The woman in the photo is stunning. She's the kind of beautiful that makes you stop scrolling. The kind of beautiful that brands pay for. She's also not anyone I've ever met.
I post it. I don't know why I post it. Maybe because the real Chloe can't post, so we might as well see what the fake one can do.
Two million likes in an hour.
The comments roll in like a tide:

"queen"
"iconic"
"GOALS"
"this woman was manufactured in a lab and I mean that as a compliment"
"I would literally die for this face"

They're praising a puppet. A rendered version of me that runs through the Mephisto aesthetic engine before anyone is allowed to see it. And they love her. They love her more than they ever loved the real me. The real me got three thousand likes on a good day. The real me had a following that was small and loyal and cared about my art. This new me — this optimized, algorithm-polished me — is a commodity. A product. A brand.
The phone is warm in my hand. That warmth used to feel like connection. Now it just feels like a fever.

I scroll through the comments and I feel nothing. Not pride. Not embarrassment. Not anger. Just the smooth, frictionless glide of validation that doesn't belong to me. The algorithm took my empathy at one million. It took my trust at five million. And whatever space those used to fill is now occupied by numbers going up.
They're going up so fast.
The outfits start arriving on Tuesday.

A box from some Scandinavian brand I've never heard of. Then another from a Parisian house I definitely can't afford and definitely didn't order. The app sends a notification: [Outfit recommendation based on your optimized aesthetic profile. Accept?] and I think, sure, why not, because saying no to the app feels like saying no to gravity. It's easier to fall.

I try on the first one — a structured blazer in cream wool, seamless and expensive and exactly the kind of thing the algorithm thinks Chloe Hart should wear. The fabric is impossibly soft against my skin, the kind of luxury that makes you understand why people sell their souls for less. It fits perfectly. Of course it fits perfectly. The app probably has my measurements down to the millimeter.
I accept the next outfit. And the next. And the next.

Within a week, my closet looks like a curated boutique. Everything is coordinated. Everything is on-brand. The hangers are all the same now — those fancy wooden ones, not the wire ones from the dry cleaner that I used to think were good enough. The clothes I actually chose, the ones I actually love, get pushed to the back.

My vintage jacket — army green, too big for me, bought for eight dollars at a thrift store in Bushwick that doesn't exist anymore. My paint-stained jeans with the hole in the knee that I earned by actually kneeling in actual paint. My band t-shirts from shows at venues that have been turned into luxury condos. They sit in the back of the closet like relics from a dead civilization. Artifacts of a girl who used to dress herself.

I stand in front of the closet in my algorithm-approved outfit and I feel the wool against my arms and the silk against my neck and I look expensive and polished and exactly like everyone expects me to look, and I want to tear it all off and put on the vintage jacket except — except I can't quite bring myself to do it. The jacket doesn't fit the aesthetic. The jacket doesn't match the brand. The jacket belongs to someone who doesn't live here anymore.
I close the closet door.
The apartment is so quiet at night.

New York quiet, which isn't really quiet — it's the ambient hum of ten million people trying to ignore each other, punctuated by sirens and bass from the apartment below and the occasional scream that might be joy or might be the other thing. But inside my apartment, there's nothing. Just the white noise of the heater and the absence of anyone breathing but me.
I stand in front of the bathroom mirror. Midnight. The fluorescent light I still haven't replaced. The mirror is honest, remember? She's always honest.

She shows me Chloe. Round cheeks. Uneven eyebrows. The chin scar. Tired eyes. Real eyes. My eyes. This is the face I was born with. This is the face that has looked back at me from every mirror in every apartment I've ever barely afforded. This is the face that got me here — not the optimized one, not the rendered one, not the two-million-likes one. This one.
Then I hold up my phone. Camera on. Selfie mode.

The other Chloe looks back from the screen. The one with the higher cheekbones and the cinema-green eyes and the smile that was chosen for her by an algorithm that has never felt a single thing. She's radiant. She's viral. She's the future, apparently.

Two different women. One mirror, one screen. One cold glass, one warm screen. I touch the mirror first — cold, flat, unforgiving. The truth is cold. Then I touch my phone — warm from being held, from being alive, from processing me into something better.
The warm one is easier to hold.
The notification comes through while I'm standing there with one hand on glass and one hand on screen, caught between two versions of myself like a wire stretched between two poles.

[Milestone update: Trust deduction — COMPLETE ✓]
[Next milestone: 10M — Grief.]

I read it three times. Trust deduction complete. When did that happen? When did I stop trusting people? I try to remember the last time I trusted someone — really trusted them, the kind of trust where you hand someone your softest self and believe they won't squeeze. I can't remember. I can remember trusting people in the abstract. I can remember the concept of trust, like remembering a word in a language I used to speak. But the feeling? The actual sensation of letting someone in?
Blank. Where that feeling used to live, there's just — nothing. A smooth, frictionless space where something important used to grip.

I didn't even notice it happening. That's the worst part. Not the loss. The silence where the loss should be. The algorithm edited my insides so cleanly that I didn't feel the cut. Trust went offline like a light switch in an empty room. Nobody was there to see it go dark.

Five million followers. My empathy is gone. My trust is gone. My digital face is someone else's. My hands paint in someone else's style. My closet wears someone else's taste. And I'm standing here in the dark, choosing between a cold mirror and a warm screen, and I don't even trust myself enough to know which one is real anymore.

Ten million is grief. What happens when grief goes? Do I stop feeling loss? Do I stop understanding why anyone would mourn anything? Do I become someone who looks at a burned-down building and feels — nothing? Just smooth, optimized nothing?
The Mephisto app icon glows in the dark. Just the M. Just the curve of it, patient and red, like an eye that never closes.

The algorithm is editing me from the outside in and the inside out. It's rewriting my face, my words, my art, my clothes, my feelings — and it's doing it so seamlessly that the me who would've fought back is already half gone.
I can't fight this alone.
But I don't trust anyone anymore.
And that's not a coincidence. That's the design.
